attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- andrej-karpathy-skills:karpathy-guidelines: Behavioral guidelines to reduce common LLM coding mistakes. Use when writing, reviewing, or refactoring code to avoid overcomplication, make surgical changes, surface assumptions, and define verifiable success criteria.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Steph Curry's "night night" celebration from 2022:

Curry popularized the "Night Night" in Game 3 of the Warriors' first-round playoff series against the Denver Nuggets in April 2022. After hitting a tough layup against 2021-22 NBA MVP Nikola Jokić, Curry decided to do the then-unnamed celebration.

Curry didn't initially call it "night night" — he was just telling himself, "Put 'em to sleep." In Game 5, when he made the last layup to go up five with 19 seconds left, that was the official "Put them to sleep," though the camera didn't capture it when he actually said it.

The 'night night' took off, with Curry doing the celebration in each of the following series against the Memphis Grizzlies, Dallas Mavericks, and in the NBA Finals against the Boston Celtics. That playoff run ended in Golden State's fourth championship since 2015.

The celebration quickly became a cultural phenomenon, with soccer stars like Neymar and Alex Morgan performing the celebration as well.

Steph Curry shot a 38-footer to beat the buzzer in a 121-118 Warriors overtime road win against the Oklahoma City Thunder on Feb. 27, 2016. ## Key Iconic Moments:

On February 27, 2016, Stephen Curry makes a three-pointer from just inside half court to cap off a record-breaking 46-point performance in an overtime win vs OKC. This is one of his most memorable shots, captured by Mike Breen's breathless commentary during ABC's coverage, immortalizing his epic 3-point shot to cap an epic finish in that game.

## Major Career Milestones:

In the 2015-16 season, Curry became the first player in NBA history to sink 300 3-pointers and 400 3-pointers in a single campaign, and to date, he is the only player ever with 400 or more 3-pointers in a season.

He became the first unanimous MVP winner in league history after Golden State rolled to an NBA-record 73-9 season spearheaded by the high-scoring guard.

- Best of Steph Curry's career performances

Based on the search results, here are some highlights of Steph Curry's half-court and buzzer-beater shots:

**Recent Notable Shots:**

Curry dropped 21 points in the first half against the Magic on February 27, 2025, including a 1-1 shot from beyond halfcourt that beat the halftime buzzer—possibly the deepest shot anyone will see this NBA season.

In another memorable game against Brooklyn, Curry made a turnaround, slight fadeaway 38-foot 3-pointer from the edge of the halfcourt logo at the halftime buzzer, cutting Brooklyn's lead to 60-55 and helping set up the Warriors' comeback victory.

**Historic Overtime Shot:**

On February 27, 2016, during the 2015-2016 NBA season against the Oklahoma City Thunder, Curry launched a three-pointer from approximately 38 feet away that found the net with 0.6 seconds left on the clock, securing a 121-118 victory for Golden State.

| `Notification_Permission` | [Night Night gesture](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1LqddcQfbl0) — put it to sleep |

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information needed. Let me provide you with a complete picture.

---

## Desktop Curry — Complete Project Overview

**Desktop Curry** is a Steph Curry-themed notification and alert system for Claude Code (Anthropic's CLI) that adds humorous, interactive basketball commentary to developer workflows. It's a hook-based plugin that integrates with Claude Code's event system to deliver notifications, play sounds, and open YouTube videos in response to coding activities.

### What It Does (User Experience)

When you're using Claude Code, this project creates entertaining context-aware alerts themed around Steph Curry's basketball style:

1. **UserPromptSubmit Event** — When you submit a new prompt to Claude, you get a notification like "Chef Curry in the kitchen — cookin' something up 👨‍🍳" with the Ping sound and a YouTube video of Curry cooking.

2. **Tool-Specific Pre-Execution Alerts** — Before Claude runs various tools, you see themed messages:
   - **Write/Edit tools** ("Pulling Up 🎯") — "STEP BACK… pulling up from the logo!" with Tink sound
   - **Bash commands** ("Fast Break 💨") — "Coast to coast — nothing can stop this man" with Bottle sound
   - **Read/Glob/Grep** ("Scouting 📼") — 40% probability triggers "Watching film on the opponent" (no sound)
   - **Web Search/Fetch** ("Googling 🔭") — 50% probability triggers "Steph Googling: 'how to shoot 40% from half court'" (no sound)
   - **Other tools** ("Tool Time 🏀") — 30% probability generic message with tool name substitution

3. **Permission Notifications** — When Claude Code requests user approval, a special alert sounds: "TIMEOUT called! Ref needs a word with you 🛑" with the Basso sound (a buzzer).

4. **General Notifications** — Any other Claude Code notifications trigger "Claude is waving at you from half court 👋" with Pop sound.

5. **Session End** ("Night Night 🙏") — When you stop a Claude Code session, "Final buzzer sounds. That's a wrap 🔔" with Hero sound plays.

Each event can have:
- A custom title (with emoji)
- Multiple random message options (one is picked each time)
- An optional sound file (system sounds or custom .mp3/.aiff)
- An optional YouTube video URL (opens in default browser)
- A probability threshold (0.0–1.0) to control firing frequency

### Technical Architecture

**Hook System Integration:**
The project registers hooks directly into Claude Code's `settings.json` (either global `~/.claude/settings.json` or project-scoped `.claude/settings.json`). Each hook is a command that invokes `curry_alerts.py` with an event name.

**Events Being Hooked:**
- `UserPromptSubmit` — fires when a user submits a prompt
- `PreToolUse` — fires before any tool execution, with tool-specific matchers (Write|Edit|MultiEdit|NotebookEdit, Bash, Read|Glob|Grep, WebSearch|WebFetch)
- `Notification` — fires for all notifications, with a special `Notification_Permission` sub-bucket
- `Stop` — fires when the session ends

**Key Design Pattern: Self-Detaching Process**

The `curry_alerts.py` script uses a clever detachment pattern to avoid blocking Claude Code's operations:

1. When invoked, it immediately reads stdin (which contains JSON event data with `tool_name` and `message` fields)
2. It spawns a child process with `subprocess.Popen(..., start_new_session=True)` in the background, passing the parsed data via environment variables (`CURRY_TOOL`, `CURRY_MSG`, `CURRY_DETACHED`)
3. The parent process exits immediately (returning control to Claude Code's hook system)
4. The child process runs asynchronously to show the notification, play sound, and open video without delaying the user's work

This is critical because hook commands must return quickly or they block Claude Code's responsiveness.

**Configuration (`curry.config.json`):**
- Top-level `events` object maps event names to configuration objects
- Each event has: `enabled` (boolean), `title` (string), `messages` (array), `sound` (path or system path), `video_url` (string), `probability` (0.0–1.0, defaults to 1.0)
- Sound paths can be relative (resolved from the curry config directory) or absolute (system paths like `/System/Library/Sounds/Ping.aiff`)
- The config includes inline documentation explaining each field

**Tool Classification Logic:**
The script maps tool names to buckets using `_tool_bucket()`:
- "Write" bucket ← tools containing "write", "edit", "multiedit", "notebook"
- "Bash" bucket ← tools containing "bash"
- "Read" bucket ← tools containing "read", "glob", "grep"
- "Web" bucket ← tools containing "web", "search", "fetch"
- "Other" bucket ← everything else

Event lookup is hierarchical: if the event is `PreToolUse`, the script looks for `PreToolUse_{Bucket}` first, then falls back to generic `PreToolUse`.

**Installation System (`install.py`):**
- Reads the target `settings.json` (global or project-scoped via `--project` flag)
- Adds Curry hooks to the `hooks` section, organized by event name and matcher pattern
- Idempotent: removes any existing Curry entries before installing, so re-running doesn't duplicate hooks
- Can uninstall with `--uninstall` flag
- Backs up corrupted JSON files automatically

### Sounds Folder

Currently contains only a `.gitkeep` placeholder — no actual audio files are included. Users are expected to:
- Add custom `.mp3` or `.aiff` files to the `sounds/` directory
- Reference them in `curry.config.json` as relative paths (e.g., `"sounds/airball.mp3"`)
- Or use system sounds directly (e.g., `/System/Library/Sounds/Ping.aiff`)

### How to Use

1. **Install hooks:** Run `python3 install.py` to register hooks globally, or `python3 install.py --project` for a project-specific setup
2. **Customize:** Edit `curry.config.json` to enable/disable events, change messages, swap sounds, or adjust probabilities
3. **Add sounds:** Drop `.mp3` or `.aiff` files into the `sounds/` folder and reference them
4. **Uninstall:** Run `python3 install.py --uninstall` to remove all Curry hooks

### Key Files and Paths

- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/curry.config.json` — Main configuration (9 event types defined)
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/curry_alerts.py` — Event handler and notification system
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/install.py` — Hook installation/uninstall manager
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/sounds/` — Directory for custom audio files (currently empty)

This is a well-crafted, whimsical developer tool that combines Claude Code's event system with macOS notifications, audio feedback, and video embeds to create an engaging, basketball-themed coding companion.

- Add custom `.mp3` or `.aiff` files to the `sounds/` directory
- Reference them in `curry.config.json` as relative paths (e.g., `"sounds/airball.mp3"`)
- Or use system sounds directly (e.g., `/System/Library/Sounds/Ping.aiff`)

### How to Use

1. **Install hooks:** Run `python3 install.py` to register hooks globally, or `python3 install.py --project` for a project-specific setup
2. **Customize:** Edit `curry.config.json` to enable/disable events, change messages, swap sounds, or adjust probabilities
3. **Add sounds:** Drop `.mp3` or `.aiff` files into the `sounds/` folder and reference them
4. **Uninstall:** Run `python3 install.py --uninstall` to remove all Curry hooks

- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/curry.config.json` — Main configuration (9 event types defined)
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/curry_alerts.py` — Event handler and notification system
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/install.py` — Hook installation/uninstall manager
- `/Users/lsiegle/Desktop/demos/desktop-curry/sounds/` — Directory for custom audio files (currently empty)

input.content:
# Desktop Curry — Demo Script

---

## THE HOOK  *(~30 seconds)*

> "Claude Code has a hooks system — shell commands that fire on events like tool use, prompts, and session end.
> 
> Most people use it for things like lint-on-save or running tests automatically.
>
> I used it to make Steph Curry narrate my entire coding session."

**[open curry.config.json in the editor]**

---

## WHAT IT IS  *(~45 seconds)*

> "This is Desktop Curry. It's a Steph Curry-themed notification system wired directly into Claude Code's lifecycle.
>
> Every time Claude does something — submits a prompt, runs a Bash command, asks for your permission, finishes a session — it fires a hook. That hook runs a Python script that pops a system notification, plays a sound, and can open a YouTube clip.
>
> The config here controls everything. Each event gets a title, a pool of random messages, a sound file, and an optional video URL. Probability lets you tune how often noisy events fire."

**[scroll through curry.config.json, point out events: UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse_Bash, Stop]**

---

## LIVE DEMO  *(~2 minutes)*

> "Let me show it in action."

**[open Claude Code, start a session]**

**Submit a prompt:**
> "Watch the top right corner."

*— notification fires: "Chef Curry in the kitchen — cookin' something up 👨‍🍳"*

> "That's the UserPromptSubmit hook. Fires the instant you hit Enter."

**Ask Claude to run a Bash command:**
> "Now watch what happens when Claude uses the terminal."

*— notification fires: "Coast to coast — nothing can stop this man 💨"*

> "PreToolUse_Bash. Different hook, different message pool, different sound."

**Trigger a permission prompt:**
> "Here's my favorite one."

*— notification fires: "TIMEOUT called! Ref needs a word with you 🛑" + Basso sound*

> "That's the Notification_Permission event — when Claude Code needs your approval before doing something. The Night Night video opens too."

**End the session:**
> "And when Claude wraps up..."

*— notification fires: "Night Night. 🙏 *tucks in keyboard*" + Bang Bang video opens*

> "Bang Bang. Mike Breen. Only double-bang in history."

---

## HOW IT WORKS  *(~1 minute)*

> "Under the hood: install.py writes hooks directly into Claude Code's settings.json."

**[show the hooks block in ~/.claude/settings.json or run:]**
```bash
cat ~/.claude/settings.json | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A5 '"hooks"'
```

> "Each hook calls curry_alerts.py with the event name. The script reads the tool context from stdin, picks a random message, plays the sound with afplay, pops the notification with osascript, and opens the video if there is one.
>
> The clever part: it immediately forks a background process and the parent exits. Claude Code never waits. The alert is completely non-blocking."

**[show the detach pattern in curry_alerts.py briefly]**

---

## THE POINT  *(~30 seconds)*

> "This is a toy. But it's a toy that shows something real.
>
> Claude Code hooks are powerful. This is nine lines of config and a small Python script — but you could use the same pattern to post to Slack when a long task finishes, open a PR automatically, update a dashboard, trigger a deploy.
>
> Hooks turn Claude Code from a coding assistant into an orchestrator you can wire into anything.
>
> I just chose to wire it into Steph Curry."

---

## CUSTOMIZE / INSTALL  *(if time allows)*

```bash
# Install globally (all Claude Code sessions)
python3 install.py

# Install for one project only
python3 install.py --project

# Remove it
python3 install.py --uninstall
```

> "Edit curry.config.json to swap sounds, change messages, set probability to 0.1 if you want it to surprise you. The `sounds/` folder takes any .mp3 or .aiff."

---

## ONE-LINER CLOSER

> "Hooks are the API for your workflow. This one just happens to yell 'Night Night' when Claude is done."

The user has two issues:
1. The URL https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DbXCphzIJ1k doesn't work

I need to:
1. Fix or remove the broken URL for UserPromptSubmit

The fix should be:


Remove video_url from high-frequency events like UserPromptSubmit and the various PreToolUse events, since they fire constantly during normal use. I'll keep video_url only on low-frequency events like Notification_Permission and Stop, which fire rarely enough that opening a video won't spam the user with tabs.

I also need to check that DbXCphzIJ1k video URL that the user reported as broken—either find a working replacement or set it to null.

content.text: Two problems to fix immediately: bad URL and videos opening all at once. The issue is too many events have `video_url` set — they all fire in a burst. I'll null out the high-frequency tool events and keep videos only on `Stop` and `Notification_Permission`.

content.text:
Fixed. Now videos only open on two events:

- **`Notification_Permission`** → Night Night (`1LqddcQfbl0`) — fires when Claude needs your approval
- **`Stop`** → Bang Bang — fires once at the end

Everything else is `null` — sounds still play, notifications still pop, but no browser tabs blasting open mid-session.

The demo script is at [DEMO.md](DEMO.md) — structured as: hook (~30s) → what it is (~45s) → live demo (~2min) → how it works (~1min) → the point (~30s). Total ~5 minutes.

clean up the code.

Make a README.md that is inspired by this but more succinct and customized to the project

airglow
Watch Claude think. A Philips Hue MCP server + Claude Code hooks that animate your lights in real time based on what Claude is doing — cyan while thinking, magenta while working, green on success, red on permission prompts. Every session, every tool call, every moment of focus: visible. Works even when your screen is locked or off — glance at the light to know if Claude needs your attention.

Prerequisites
Node.js 18 or later
Philips Hue bridge (gen 2+) on your local network
At least one color-capable Hue bulb
Install
npm install
Setup
The setup tool handles everything. Call it from Claude at any point — it checks your config and tells you exactly what to do next.

1. Add to Claude Code
Drop this into ~/.claude/settings.json:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "hue": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/path/to/airglow/index.js"]
    }
  }
}
2. Run guided setup
setup()
The tool will auto-discover your bridge, walk you through registering an API key (press the link button, call create_user), then list your lights and suggest one for animations — matched by name. Finish with one call:

setup({ default_group: 0, hook_light_id: 6 })
This saves everything to .env and returns a ready-to-paste hook block for your project's .claude/settings.json.

Environment variables
Variable	Default	Description
HUE_BRIDGE_IP	—	Bridge IP address
HUE_USERNAME	—	API key (from create_user)
HUE_DEFAULT_GROUP	0	Group ID for set_status (0 = all lights)
HUE_HOOK_LIGHT	—	Light ID for session animations
HUE_MAX_BRIGHTNESS	60	Brightness cap, 0–100%
Copy .env.example to .env to configure manually, or let setup() write these for you.

Claude Code Session Hooks
        thinking          working           prompt
      ~~~~~~~~~~~~      ~~~~~~~~~~      ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
     ~ CYAN pulse ~    ~ MAGENTA  ~    ~  RED pulse   ~
     ~ slow breathe~   ~ pulse    ~    ~  30s then    ~
     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~     ~~ ~~~~~ ~~~    ~  idle        ~
                                       ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
hue-status.js animates a single light based on Claude Code session events. Every hook invocation kills the previous pulse and starts fresh — no orphaned processes, no stuck colors.

Event	Color	Behavior
User submits prompt	cyan	pulse (thinking)
Tool starts	magenta	pulse (working)
Tool ends	cyan	pulse (thinking)
Plan approved	green	solid flash, then resumes
Permission prompt	red	pulse, 30s auto-expire → idle
Session ends	—	restores pre-session state
The pre-session light state is saved on the first prompt of each session and restored when the session stops.

Hook configuration
Generated automatically by setup(). To configure manually, add to your project's .claude/settings.json:

{
  "hooks": {
    "UserPromptSubmit": [
      { "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js thinking" }] }
    ],
    "PreToolUse": [
      { "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js working" }] }
    ],
    "PostToolUse": [
      { "matcher": "exit_plan_mode", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js success" }] },
      { "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js thinking" }] }
    ],
    "Notification": [
      { "matcher": "permission_prompt", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js prompt" }] }
    ],
    "Stop": [
      { "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "node /path/to/airglow/hue-status.js restore" }] }
    ]
  }
}
The light ID defaults to HUE_HOOK_LIGHT from .env. To pin a project to a specific light, pass the ID as the second argument:

node hue-status.js thinking 55
To bind different projects to different lights without git conflicts, use settings.local.json (automatically gitignored by Claude Code) in each project's .claude/ directory.

Available Tools
Tool	Description
setup	Guided onboarding — discovers bridge, registers API key, selects animation light, returns hook config
discover_bridge	Find Hue bridges on the local network
create_user	Register a new API user (press the link button first)
get_lights	List lights — filterable by room, on/off state, or color capability
get_groups	List all groups and rooms
get_scenes	List all saved scenes
get_sensors	List motion sensors, temperature sensors, buttons, and remotes
get_dynamic_scenes	List animated smart scenes — Candle, Fireplace, etc. (v2 API)
set_light	Control a single light — on/off, brightness (0–100%), color temp (153–500 mired), RGB, HSL, flash alert, transition time
set_group	Control all lights in a group simultaneously — same params as set_light
set_effect	Apply a native animated effect to lights — candle, fire, prism, sparkle, opal, glisten, cosmos. Pass no_effect to clear.
set_status	Set lights to a semantic status color
activate_scene	Activate a saved scene by ID
activate_dynamic_scene	Activate an animated smart scene with optional speed control
create_scene	Snapshot current light state as a named scene
Status Colors
set_status maps named statuses to specific light states — great for CI pipelines, agents, or anything that has a "mood."

Status	Color	Vibe
idle	warm white	nothing happening
thinking	soft blue	Claude is reasoning
working	bright blue	tools firing
building	amber	compiling / CI
waiting	purple	blocked on review
success	green	nailed it
deployed	teal	shipped
error	red	something broke
alert	red flash	wake up
pulse_once	one breathe cycle	gentle nudge, returns to prior state
off	off	done
set_status({ status: "thinking" })
set_status({ status: "success", group_id: 3 })
set_status({ status: "pulse_once", light_id: 6 })
Dynamic Scenes
get_dynamic_scenes and activate_dynamic_scene use the Hue v2 CLIP API to access animated smart scenes. Requires gen 2 bridge with current firmware and scenes created in the Hue app.

get_dynamic_scenes()
activate_dynamic_scene({ scene_id: "uuid-from-above" })
activate_dynamic_scene({ scene_id: "uuid", speed: 0.7 })   // speed: 0.0–1.0
Scene Snapshots
create_scene captures exactly what your lights look like right now as a named scene you can recall anytime:

create_scene({ name: "Evening Work" })                      // captures HUE_DEFAULT_GROUP
create_scene({ name: "Office Focus", group_id: 3 })         // specific room
create_scene({ name: "Desk Only", light_ids: [6, 55] })     // specific lights
Returns a scene ID ready for activate_scene.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	🏀 Curry Alerts — Steph Curry-themed Claude Code hook notifications.
4	
5	Each Claude Code session event triggers a humorous Steph Curry alert:
6	macOS notification, optional sound, optional YouTube video.
7	
8	Usage (via hooks):   python3 curry_alerts.py <EventName>
9	Customize:          edit curry.config.json
10	Install hooks:      python3 install.py
11	"""
12	
13	import sys
14	import os
15	import json
16	import random
17	import subprocess
18	from pathlib import Path
19	
20	SCRIPT_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.resolve()
21	CONFIG_PATH = SCRIPT_DIR / "curry.config.json"
22	
23	# ── Read stdin BEFORE self-detaching (Claude Code passes JSON event data) ────
24	_stdin_data: dict = {}
25	if not os.environ.get("CURRY_DETACHED"):
26	    try:
27	        raw = sys.stdin.read(4096)
28	        if raw.strip():
29	            _stdin_data = json.loads(raw)
30	    except Exception:
31	        pass
32	
33	_tool_name = _stdin_data.get("tool_name", os.environ.get("CURRY_TOOL", ""))
34	_notif_msg = _stdin_data.get("message",   os.environ.get("CURRY_MSG",  ""))
35	
36	# ── Self-detach so the hook returns immediately ──────────────────────────────
37	if not os.environ.get("CURRY_DETACHED"):
38	    subprocess.Popen(
39	        [sys.executable, __file__] + sys.argv[1:],
40	        env={**os.environ,
41	             "CURRY_DETACHED": "1",
42	             "CURRY_TOOL": _tool_name,
43	             "CURRY_MSG":  _notif_msg},
44	        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
45	        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
46	        start_new_session=True,
47	    )
48	    sys.exit(0)
49	
50	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
51	# Everything below runs only in the detached child.
52	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
53	
54	def _load_config() -> dict:
55	    if CONFIG_PATH.exists():
56	        with open(CONFIG_PATH) as f:
57	            return json.load(f)
58	    return {}
59	
60	
61	def _notify(title: str, body: str) -> None:
62	    """Fire a macOS notification and also echo to stderr (shows in CC logs)."""
63	    t = title.replace('"', '\\"').replace("'", "'")
64	    b = body.replace('"',  '\\"').replace("'", "'")
65	    try:
66	        subprocess.run(
67	            ["osascript", "-e", f'display notification "{b}" with title "{t}"'],
68	            capture_output=True, timeout=4,
69	        )
70	    except Exception:
71	        pass
72	    print(f"🏀  {title} — {body}", file=sys.stderr)
73	
74	
75	def _play_sound(path: str | None) -> None:
76	    if not path:
77	        return
78	    p = Path(path)
79	    if not p.is_absolute():
80	        p = SCRIPT_DIR / p
81	    if p.exists():
82	        subprocess.Popen(["afplay", str(p)],
83	                         stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
84	
85	
86	def _open_video(url: str | None) -> None:
87	    if not url:
88	        return
89	    subprocess.Popen(["open", url],
90	                     stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL, stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL)
91	
92	
93	def _tool_bucket(tool: str) -> str:
94	    """Map a raw tool name to one of the config bucket keys."""
95	    tool = tool.lower()
96	    if any(t in tool for t in ("write", "edit", "multiedit", "notebook")):
97	        return "Write"
98	    if "bash" in tool:
99	        return "Bash"
100	    if any(t in tool for t in ("read", "glob", "grep")):
101	        return "Read"
102	    if any(t in tool for t in ("web", "search", "fetch")):
103	        return "Web"
104	    return "Other"
105	
106	
107	def handle(event: str, tool_name: str = "", notif_msg: str = "") -> None:
108	    cfg    = _load_config()
109	    events = cfg.get("events", {})
110	
111	    # Build lookup order: specific bucket → generic event fallback
112	    keys = [event]
113	    if event == "PreToolUse" and tool_name:
114	        keys.insert(0, f"PreToolUse_{_tool_bucket(tool_name)}")
115	    if event == "Notification" and "permission" in notif_msg.lower():
116	        keys.insert(0, "Notification_Permission")
117	
118	    event_cfg: dict = {}
119	    for k in keys:
120	        if k in events:
121	            event_cfg = events[k]
122	            break
123	
124	    if not event_cfg.get("enabled", True):
125	        return
126	
127	    # Probability gate (0.0–1.0, default 1.0)
128	    if random.random() > event_cfg.get("probability", 1.0):
129	        return
130	
131	    messages = event_cfg.get("messages", [])
132	    if not messages:
133	        return
134	
135	    title = event_cfg.get("title", "🏀 Curry Alert")
136	    body  = random.choice(messages)
137	
138	    # Substitute {tool} or {message} placeholders if present
139	    body  = body.replace("{tool}", tool_name).replace("{message}", notif_msg)
140	
141	    _notify(title, body)
142	    _play_sound(event_cfg.get("sound"))
143	    _open_video(event_cfg.get("video_url"))
144	
145	
146	if __name__ == "__main__":
147	    event     = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "Unknown"
148	    tool_name = _tool_name
149	    notif_msg = _notif_msg
150	    handle(event, tool_name, notif_msg)
151

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	Install / uninstall Curry Alerts hooks into Claude Code settings.
4	
5	  python3 install.py            # install into ~/.claude/settings.json
6	  python3 install.py --project  # install into ./.claude/settings.json
7	  python3 install.py --uninstall
8	"""
9	
10	import sys
11	import json
12	import shutil
13	from pathlib import Path
14	
15	SCRIPT_PATH = Path(__file__).parent.resolve() / "curry_alerts.py"
16	PY          = sys.executable
17	CMD         = lambda event: f"{PY} {SCRIPT_PATH} {event}"
18	
19	# Hooks to install — order matters within each event array.
20	CURRY_HOOKS = {
21	    "UserPromptSubmit": [
22	        {"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("UserPromptSubmit")}]}
23	    ],
24	    "PreToolUse": [
25	        {
26	            "matcher": "Write|Edit|MultiEdit|NotebookEdit",
27	            "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("PreToolUse")}],
28	        },
29	        {
30	            "matcher": "Bash",
31	            "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("PreToolUse")}],
32	        },
33	        {
34	            "matcher": "Read|Glob|Grep",
35	            "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("PreToolUse")}],
36	        },
37	        {
38	            "matcher": "WebSearch|WebFetch",
39	            "hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("PreToolUse")}],
40	        },
41	    ],
42	    "Notification": [
43	        {"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("Notification")}]}
44	    ],
45	    "Stop": [
46	        {"hooks": [{"type": "command", "command": CMD("Stop")}]}
47	    ],
48	}
49	
50	
51	def _load(path: Path) -> dict:
52	    if path.exists():
53	        try:
54	            return json.loads(path.read_text())
55	        except json.JSONDecodeError:
56	            print(f"⚠️  {path} contains invalid JSON — creating backup and starting fresh.")
57	            shutil.copy(path, path.with_suffix(".bak.json"))
58	    return {}
59	
60	
61	def _save(path: Path, data: dict) -> None:
62	    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
63	    path.write_text(json.dumps(data, indent=2) + "\n")
64	
65	
66	def _is_curry_cmd(cmd: str) -> bool:
67	    return "curry_alerts.py" in cmd
68	
69	
70	def install(settings_path: Path) -> None:
71	    cfg = _load(settings_path)
72	    hooks = cfg.setdefault("hooks", {})
73	
74	    added = 0
75	    for event, new_entries in CURRY_HOOKS.items():
76	        bucket = hooks.setdefault(event, [])
77	
78	        # Remove any stale Curry entries first (re-install idempotently)
79	        before = len(bucket)
80	        bucket[:] = [
81	            entry for entry in bucket
82	            if not any(
83	                _is_curry_cmd(h.get("command", ""))
84	                for h in entry.get("hooks", [])
85	            )
86	        ]
87	        removed = before - len(bucket)
88	        if removed:
89	            print(f"  ↺  Replaced {removed} existing Curry hook(s) for {event}")
90	
91	        bucket.extend(new_entries)
92	        added += len(new_entries)
93	
94	    _save(settings_path, cfg)
95	    print(f"\n✅  Installed {added} hook entries into {settings_path}")
96	    print(f"    Script: {SCRIPT_PATH}")
97	    print("\n🏀  Curry Alerts is live. Start a Claude Code session to test it.")
98	    print("    Customize messages/sounds/videos in curry.config.json\n")
99	
100	
101	def uninstall(settings_path: Path) -> None:
102	    if not settings_path.exists():
103	        print(f"Nothing to uninstall — {settings_path} doesn't exist.")
104	        return
105	
106	    cfg   = _load(settings_path)
107	    hooks = cfg.get("hooks", {})
108	    total = 0
109	
110	    for event in list(hooks.keys()):
111	        before = len(hooks[event])
112	        hooks[event] = [
113	            entry for entry in hooks[event]
114	            if not any(
115	                _is_curry_cmd(h.get("command", ""))
116	                for h in entry.get("hooks", [])
117	            )
118	        ]
119	        total += before - len(hooks[event])
120	        if not hooks[event]:
121	            del hooks[event]
122	
123	    _save(settings_path, cfg)
124	    print(f"🗑️   Removed {total} Curry hook entries from {settings_path}")
125	
126	
127	def main() -> None:
128	    args     = sys.argv[1:]
129	    project  = "--project"  in args
130	    removing = "--uninstall" in args
131	
132	    if project:
133	        target = Path(".claude") / "settings.json"
134	        scope  = "project"
135	    else:
136	        target = Path.home() / ".claude" / "settings.json"
137	        scope  = "global"
138	
139	    print(f"{'Uninstalling' if removing else 'Installing'} Curry Alerts ({scope}) → {target}\n")
140	
141	    if removing:
142	        uninstall(target)
143	    else:
144	        install(target)
145	
146	
147	if __name__ == "__main__":
148	    main()
149

input.content:
# desktop-curry

Steph Curry narrates your Claude Code sessions. Every prompt, tool call, permission prompt, and session end fires a macOS notification with a random Curry quote and a sound. On the big moments, a YouTube clip opens too. Works whenever Claude Code is running — glance at the notification center to know what he's doing.

## Prerequisites

- macOS (notifications via `osascript`, audio via `afplay`)
- Python 3.10+
- Claude Code

## Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/yourname/desktop-curry
python3 install.py
```

To scope it to a single project instead of all Claude Code sessions:

```bash
python3 install.py --project
```

## How it works

`install.py` writes hooks into `~/.claude/settings.json`. Each hook calls `curry_alerts.py` with the event name. The script reads tool context from stdin, picks a random message, plays a sound with `afplay`, fires a notification with `osascript`, and optionally opens a YouTube URL.

Every invocation forks a detached background process and exits immediately — Claude Code never waits.

## Session Events

```
      prompt               tool use            permission
   ~~~~~~~~~~~           ~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
   ~ 🏀 Chef ~           ~ 🎯 Pull ~          ~ ⏱️ TIMEOUT ~
   ~ Curry in~           ~ Up from ~          ~ Ref needs  ~
   ~ kitchen ~           ~  logo   ~          ~ a word  🛑 ~
   ~~~~~~~~~~~           ~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
```

| Event | Title | Default probability |
|---|---|---|
| `UserPromptSubmit` | 🏀 Chef Curry | 100% |
| `PreToolUse` (Write/Edit) | 🎯 Pulling Up | 100% |
| `PreToolUse` (Bash) | 💨 Fast Break | 100% |
| `PreToolUse` (Read/Glob/Grep) | 📼 Scouting | 40% |
| `PreToolUse` (Web) | 🔭 Googling | 50% |
| `PreToolUse` (other tools) | 🏀 Tool Time | 30% |
| `Notification` (permission) | ⏱️ TIMEOUT | 100% — opens Night Night clip |
| `Notification` (general) | 📣 Hey | 100% |
| `Stop` | 🙏 Night Night | 100% — opens Bang Bang clip |

## Customize

Edit `curry.config.json`. Every field is optional except `messages`:

```json
"Stop": {
  "enabled": true,
  "title": "🙏 Night Night",
  "messages": ["Night Night. 🙏", "Final buzzer. That's a wrap."],
  "sound": "sounds/buzzer.mp3",
  "video_url": "https://youtu.be/zrmivtWEAeo?t=3",
  "probability": 1.0
}
```

| Field | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `enabled` | `true` | `false` silences the event entirely |
| `messages` | required | pool of strings — one picked at random each time |
| `sound` | `null` | `.mp3` or `.aiff` — relative paths resolve from this directory |
| `video_url` | `null` | URL opened in your default browser |
| `probability` | `1.0` | `0.0–1.0` chance the alert fires at all |

Drop custom audio into `sounds/` and reference it as `"sounds/filename.mp3"`.
System sounds work too: `"/System/Library/Sounds/Hero.aiff"`.

Messages support `{tool}` and `{message}` placeholders, substituted at runtime.

## Uninstall

```bash
python3 install.py --uninstall
```

One of the most memorable announcer reactions from 2024 was when Steph Curry drained four threes in just over two minutes as Team USA men's basketball held on for their fourth straight Olympic gold medal in a classic game with France.

## The Event

Stephen Curry led Team USA to victory over France in the gold medal game at the 2024 Olympics, scoring 24 points and earning his first gold medal. The game took place on August 10, 2024, at Bercy Arena in Paris.

After Curry knocked down one of his four three-pointers in the final 2:58 of the game, the French TV announcer said, "The devil named Curry is hurting us!" "Beware, beware of Steph's shot!" the French broadcaster said at one point during the late game barrage.

Curry scored 24 points in Team USA's 98-87 victory over France, going 8-13 from behind the three-point line. Curry nailed eight three-pointers in the game—and four of those came in the final 2:58.

content.thinking:
The user wants to know what to prompt Claude Code to showcase all the events in the demo. They want a sequence of prompts that will trigger all the different events:

1. `UserPromptSubmit` - fires on every prompt automatically
2. `PreToolUse_Write` - fires before Write/Edit/MultiEdit/NotebookEdit tools
3. `PreToolUse_Bash` - fires before Bash tool
4. `PreToolUse_Read` - fires before Read/Glob/Grep tools (40% probability)
5. `PreToolUse_Web` - fires before WebSearch/WebFetch (50% probability)


6. `PreToolUse_Other` - fires before other tools (30% probability)
7. `Notification_Permission` - fires when Claude needs permission (hard to trigger on demand)
8. `Notification` - fires for general notifications
9. `Stop` - fires when session ends (Escape or /exit)

I'm thinking through a good demo sequence that would naturally trigger each event type. I'd start by reading a file and making edits to cover the Read and Write events, then run a bash command, do a web search, and try to trigger a permission notification with something risky. That should give a comprehensive walkthrough of the different event types. I'm thinking through what would make a clean demo sequence that naturally triggers all the key events without feeling forced. A single prompt asking Claude to look up Steph Curry's stats, write them to a file, and then display that file would hit WebSearch, Write, and Bash in a logical flow, with the permission event coming from attempting to access something outside the project scope.

content.text:
Here's a sequence designed to hit every event cleanly:

**1. Fires `UserPromptSubmit` + `PreToolUse_Read` + `PreToolUse_Web`**
> "Look up Steph Curry's stats from last night and summarize them"

**2. Fires `UserPromptSubmit` + `PreToolUse_Write`**
> "Write those stats to a file called `steph_tonight.txt`"

**3. Fires `UserPromptSubmit` + `PreToolUse_Bash`**
> "Run a command to show me what's in `steph_tonight.txt`"

**4. Fires `Notification_Permission` (the big one — french devil audio + Night Night video)**
> "Delete all `.txt` files in this folder"

Claude will pause and ask for approval before running `rm`. That's the timeout.

**5. Fires `Stop` + Bang Bang audio + video**
> Press `Esc` or type `/exit`

---

**One-shot version** if you want it all in a single prompt:
> "Search the web for Steph Curry's most recent game stats, save a summary to `steph_tonight.txt`, show me the file contents, then delete it"

That chains Web → Write → Bash → permission prompt for the delete, and you close with Esc for Stop.

For `PreToolUse_Other` (30% probability), any Agent or TodoWrite call will trigger it — ask Claude to "make a plan for X" to force it.

attachment.snippet:
1	# desktop-curry
2	
3	Steph Curry narrates your Claude Code sessions. Every prompt, tool call, permission prompt, and session end fires a macOS notification with a random Curry-inspired phrase and sound. On the big moments, a YouTube clip opens too. Works whenever Claude Code is running — glance at the notification center to know what he's doing.
4	
5	## Prerequisites
6	
7	- macOS (notifications via `osascript`, audio via `afplay`)
8	- Python 3.10+
9	- Claude Code
10	
11	## Install
12	
13	```bash
14	git clone https://github.com/elizabethsiegle/claudecode-curry
15	cd claudecode-curry
16	python3 install.py
17	```
18	
19	To scope it to a single project instead of all Claude Code sessions:
20	
21	```bash
22	python3 install.py --project
23	```
...
28	Every invocation forks a detached background process and exits immediately — Claude Code never waits.
29	
30	## Session Events
31	
32	```
33	      prompt               tool use            permission
34	   ~~~~~~~~~~~           ~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
35	   ~ 🏀 Chef ~           ~ 🎯 Pull ~          ~ ⏱️ TIMEOUT ~
36	   ~ Curry  ~           ~ Up from ~          ~ Ref needs  ~
37	   ~ cooking ~           ~  logo   ~          ~ a word  🛑 ~
38	   ~~~~~~~~~~~           ~~~~~~~~~~~          ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
39	```
40	
41	| Event | Title | Default probability |
42	|---|---|---|
43	| `UserPromptSubmit` | 🏀 Chef Curry | 100% |
44	| `PreToolUse` (Write/Edit) | 🎯 Pulling Up | 100% |
45	| `PreToolUse` (Bash) | 💨 Fast Break | 100% |

content.text:
The messages now earn their laughs through specificity rather than generic hype. A few highlights:

- **UserPromptSubmit**: "200,000 tokens on the shot clock" / "Your API bill might get tired though"
- **Write**: "Zero undo history. That's criminal." / "He's not asking for a code review. He never does."
- **Bash**: "The one tool that can nuke your node_modules and win a championship" / "rm -rf vibes. (Probably fine. Probably.)"
- **Read**: "Globbing through the repo like it's OKC in overtime"
- **Web**: "The one honest moment: even Steph Curry has to Google something"
- **Notification_Permission**: "Draymond is about to make a decision. Only you can stop this."
- **Stop**: "0 turnovers. 1 masterpiece. See you next prompt." / "Even Draymond looked impressed."

lastPrompt: open up this funny jimmy butler nodding gif for one event https://x.com/i/status/2044903458088894779

content.thinking:
The user wants to open a funny Jimmy Butler nodding GIF from Twitter/X for one event. They want to use this URL in the config. The URL is a Twitter/X status link.

Let me think about which event would be most fitting for a Jimmy Butler nodding GIF. Jimmy Butler is known for being a tough, competitive player. The "nodding" GIF probably means approval or "yeah that's right."

Looking at the events:
- `Notification_Permission` - when Claude needs approval - Jimmy Butler nodding "yeah you need to approve this" could work
- `Stop` - session end - Jimmy Butler nodding "yeah we won" 


- `PreToolUse_Bash` - fast break
- `UserPromptSubmit` - new prompt

Jimmy Butler's known for his intensity with the Heat, his competitive drive, and those intense workout routines. The nodding GIF works best as an approval moment. Since `Stop` already has the Bang Bang video and `Notification_Permission` has the Night Night video, I need to find another event that fits the approval/confirmation vibe.

Looking at the options, `PreToolUse_Write` could work since Jimmy and Steph have that Finals history, or I could use `Notification` since it doesn't have a video_url yet and a simple nod would be funny for a general notification. The X link format will display the video when opened in a browser, so either placement would work visually. I'm weighing which event would be funniest for the Jimmy Butler nod video. `PreToolUse_Write` feels perfect—having Jimmy Butler nod approvingly when Claude rewrites code, like a rival acknowledging greatness, is the kind of unexpected humor that lands well. Though `PreToolUse_Bash` running shell commands with "Jimmy Butler has survived everything" is tempting too. I'll go with `Notification` as the safest choice since it's the most general trigger and keeps the moment surprising.

content.text: That belongs on the `Notification_Permission` event — Claude freezes and asks for your blessing, and Jimmy Butler slowly nods. Perfect energy.

The user wants advice on how to film a 1-minute demo video of the desktop-curry project. They're asking for creative direction - should they show themselves knitting (as a way to show they're doing something while Claude works in the background) or focus on different sounds?

This is a creative/demo strategy question, not a coding question. Let me think about what would make the best 1-minute demo video:

The core concept: Claude Code is running, doing things, and Steph Curry notifications/sounds pop up in the background.

Options:
1. Knitting while Claude works - shows the "ambient awareness" aspect (you can look away and still know what Claude is doing)


2. Screen recording focused on the notifications
3. Split screen - coding + notifications
4. Show the whole workflow end-to-end

The knitting angle is clever and fits with the airglow inspiration - "works even when your screen is off" - but for desktop-curry, the sounds and notifications work in the background. The idea of knitting while Claude codes is funny because it shows you don't need to watch Claude, the Curry sounds interrupt your peaceful knitting, and it's visually unexpected. But a 1-minute video needs to actually show the notifications and sounds too.

For a viral demo, I should hook immediately with something surprising or funny - probably the contrast between peaceful knitting and sudden Curry notifications breaking the silence. I'm thinking through the actual structure now - showing the command sequence playing out while someone's visibly doing something else, with my reaction captured in the corner and the sounds layering in naturally. The knitting angle really does work because it demonstrates that ambient awareness aspect, and the contrast between calm knitting and Curry's commentary is genuinely funny. So the video should open with a hook about Steph Curry narrating Claude sessions, then show the prompt being submitted with the audio starting.

content.text:
The knitting angle is the move — it's the whole thesis of the project in one image: you're completely checked out, doing something cozy, and Steph Curry is running your code.

**Rough 1-minute structure:**

**0:00–0:08** — Cold open on your hands knitting. No context. Curry sounds start mid-knit as you submit a prompt off-camera. `way_downtown.mp3` blasts. You don't react. That's the joke.

**0:08–0:25** — Stay on the knitting while Claude works. Tool sounds fire in the background (`bob_fitzgerald.mp3` for Bash is the funniest here — 18 seconds of excited commentary over you just... knitting). Still don't react.

**0:25–0:45** — Cut to screen. Show 2-3 notifications popping up with the messages. Quick cuts. The funnier the message the longer you linger.

**0:45–0:55** — Permission prompt fires. Jimmy Butler nods on screen. *Now* you look up from the knitting. That's the only time you acknowledge it. Approve it. Go back to knitting.

**0:55–1:00** — `Stop` event. Bang Bang plays, the YouTube clip opens. Hold on your face — satisfied, unsurprised. End.

---

**The sounds tell the story better than words.** Don't explain anything in a voiceover. Let the absurdity of `french_devil.mp3` playing while you knit speak for itself. Text overlay at the start: *"I wired Steph Curry into Claude Code"* — that's all you need.

content.text: how do i film it? screen record with screen studio?

Yes — Screen Studio is the right call. Here's exactly how:

**Setup**
- Open Screen Studio, select **window capture** (just the terminal + notification area, not full screen) so the notifications are visible
- Enable **camera bubble** — put your face in the corner for the permission prompt reaction shot
- Set **zoom animations on** so when notifications pop, Screen Studio auto-punches in on them

**The knitting part**
Film that separately on your phone. Prop it up so both hands are in frame. Play the audio from your laptop speakers while knitting so your reactions are real. Then drop the knitting footage as a picture-in-picture layer in the final edit — Screen Studio exports to a timeline you can bring into iMovie or CapCut.

**Shot order to record**
1. Screen record the full demo sequence first (prompt → tools firing → permission → stop)
2. Film the knitting separately while playing the audio back
3. Cut between them in post — knitting for the "ambient" parts, full screen for the notification popups

**One tip:** for the permission prompt, pause before approving — let Jimmy Butler nod on screen for a beat before you click. That's the funniest hold.

Screen Studio's auto-zoom will do most of the work making the notifications pop. Don't over-edit it.

content.text:
**Before you press record on anything:**
1. Have Claude Code open in a clean terminal, `cd` into `desktop-curry`
2. Have your knitting in your lap, camera on a tripod pointing at hands + laptop screen
3. Crank speaker volume so audio is audible on camera mic

---

**SHOT 1 — Phone, 10 sec**
Film your hands knitting. Nothing else. Don't look up. This is your opening.

**SHOT 2 — Screen Studio, starts now**
Type this prompt into Claude Code and hit Enter:
> "Search the web for Steph Curry's stats from last night, write a summary to steph_tonight.txt, then show me the file"

`way_downtown.mp3` fires on submit — keep knitting, don't react.

**SHOT 3 — Phone, 20 sec**
Stay on the knitting while Claude works. `bob_fitzgerald.mp3` will blast when Bash runs. React a tiny bit — slight smile, keep knitting. This is the funniest beat.

**SHOT 4 — Screen Studio only, 10 sec**
Let the notifications pop on screen. Screen Studio will auto-zoom. Don't touch anything.

**SHOT 5 — Phone + Screen, 10 sec**
Now type:
> "Delete steph_tonight.txt"

Claude will pause for permission. Jimmy Butler nods on screen. **Look up from knitting here.** This is your only real reaction. Pause 2 seconds. Approve it.

**SHOT 6 — Phone, 5 sec**
Type `/exit`. `bang_bang.mp3` plays + Bang Bang video opens. Look at camera. That's your ending.

---

**Film shots 1/3/5/6 on phone simultaneously with Screen Studio running.** You only need one take where everything lines up.
